title: Soft-cover sewn journal
subtitle: A flexible cover journal sewn through-the-cover in three or five signatures
excerpt: A soft-cover journal is the simplest fully-sewn structure: a stack of folded signatures is sewn directly through a single wrapped cover with a continuous thread. There are no boards, no glue, and no case, the whole book is completed in one sewing session.
sourceNotes: Keith A. Smith, Non-Adhesive Binding: Books without Paste or Glue (1990, Sigma Foundation). The through-cover sewing structure described here follows Smith's Exposed Spine / Through-Cover construction.

glossaryTerms.definition: The linking stitch worked at the head and tail of each signature as you move from one to the next. It is a simple loop under the thread of the previous signature that locks the two together.
A soft cover formed from a single sheet folded so it wraps the text block, one fold becomes the spine, the two wings become front and back boards.

content.text: This is the binding to learn first. A soft-cover sewn journal has no adhesive, no boards to cut to exact dimensions, and opens completely flat from the first page to the last. The
is a single piece of heavy cartridge or thin card folded to fit the text block. The whole book is done when the last

attrs.heading: Materials for one A5 soft-cover journal

items.name: Cartridge paper, 90 gsm, A4
items.qty: 5 sheets per signature, fold each A4 sheet to A5 to make a 4-leaf, 8-page signature; 5 sheets make one signature of 20 pages. Use 3-5 signatures for a thin journal or up to 8 for a thick one.

items.name: Cartridge paper or thin card, 220 gsm, A4 landscape
Waxed linen bookbinding thread, 4-cord
items.qty: Approx. 2.5 m for 5 signatures, or thread length = signatures × (spine height + 100 mm)

items.name: Bookbinding needle, blunt with large eye

content.text: Fold each A4 sheet firmly in half to A5 with a bone folder. Nest five folded sheets together to make one signature, pressing the nested bundle under a flat board for two minutes. The outermost fold is the spine of that signature. Make as many signatures as the thickness you want.

Stack all your signatures together with the cover folded around them. Use a pencil to mark three or five evenly spaced sewing stations along the spine: one at each end 10 mm from the head and tail, and the remaining stations evenly between. Open each signature in turn and pierce cleanly through the spine fold at each mark with the bookbinding awl. Pierce the cover at the same stations.

Begin with the first (innermost) signature inside the cover. Thread the needle and enter from outside at the tail kettle station, leaving a 60 mm tail. Sew through each interior station, exit at the head, and link the thread around the cover spine with a kettle stitch. Add each subsequent signature by entering its tail station, sewing to the head, and linking with a kettle stitch to the previous signature. On the last signature, exit at the tail and tie the thread to the starting tail with two half hitches.

If the page edges are uneven, trim the foredge with a craft knife against a metal ruler held firmly. A single clean pass is better than multiple light cuts. The cover should overhang the text block by 2-3 mm at head, tail, and foredge, trim the cover if it does not.

The same through-cover structure works with a leather cover instead of card, giving a decades-lasting journal from a single afternoon. Add a leather cord tie, a small pocket inside the back cover, or bookbinding-thread headbands at head and tail for a more finished result.
